That said, a lot of cream is moving from butter plants and into the manufacturing of other seasonal staples, particularly cream cheese processing, as seasonal demand from those manufacturers continues to grow. Despite the storms over a week ago, farmers in the upper Midwest have had some optimally dry weather to harvest silage this week. As row crop harvest is in its early stages, mild and dry weather forecasts are being welcomed.

Some Midwestern dairy farmers reported their August figures. They relay January through August milk checks are better than they have been for a number of years. Production, at least per cow, and components have been notably above the previous years' average, as well. All that said, milk availability is somewhat snug in the region for processing. Bottling demand from within and outside of the region (the Southeast) is pulling from the overall milk pool. Clearly, these next few days will not see as much milk/cream moving into the Southeast as Hurricane Ian's impact is currently taking place. Cheesemakers in the region continue to report somewhat tight milk supplies and a lot simply say offers have been muted these past few weeks. Spot prices this week were the same as last week's flat Class to $1 over Class III. Butter makers in the region say that cream opened up some this week. Reported multiples decreased on the lower end of the range. Still, more cream cheese, eggnog, and other Q4 holiday staples are being processed at likely their peak levels of the year for these next few weeks. Cream availability has been variant from one week to the next, and this week is no different. In the Dakotas, hay demand is strong, particularly for higher quality dairy hay, but third cutting was light, at best, according to reports. Wisconsin crop conditions and fieldwork are moving along somewhat smoother, as the strong rains two weeks ago gave corn some much needed moisture. Corn harvest in Wisconsin is within a few days of the previous years' timeframe.

Milk production is starting to increase in the region as fall weather has begun to improve cow comfort, despite some warmer temperatures remaining in the South Central area during the daylight hours. Weather patterns have been generally kind to harvesters in the Midwest. The same can be said for winter wheat farmers who are looking to plant, as some much needed rainfall moved through the southern Central area of the region and more is expected in the near-term forecast.

Milk production in the region is continuing to increase week by week. Farmer contacts say cow comfort is nearly ideal, as fall weather in many parts of the region has been noted as mild. That being said, the upper Midwest has started to experience some wintry mixes, along with below-freezing temperatures throughout the upcoming weekend. Still, both producers and processors have reported an uptick on both milk and milkfat in recent weeks. Class I demand is seasonally hearty. After schools reopened in the late summer/early fall, bottlers' orders jumped, as expected. Since then, bottling has been generally steady, and in the past month, orders for retailers ahead of the holidays have grown. Cheesemakers say spot milk is generally balanced. A number of cheesemaking contacts say they are running full 7-day workweek schedules, as slight discounts to slight premiums were reported for spot milk loads this week. A few Class III contacts expect little to no change in milk availability moving into the holiday week, despite some plants closing down an extra day or two. Cream availability has grown. Butter makers report they are finding cream within the region at multiples below 1.20. Ice cream manufacturing has slowed notably, which many contacts expected. Butter plant managers say they are churning as much cream as possible, as stores are not necessarily loose ahead of the end-of-year holiday. Corn harvests, for grain, are nearing completion in most of the upper Midwest.

The agreeable weather for corn harvest has brought on some adequate silage for dairy farmers in the upper Midwest. They say this will assist in the current trend of strengthening component levels when comparing year-to-date figures to previous years. Less-than-agreeable weather in Central/Southern areas of the region has farmers there concerned regarding winter wheat growth. There has been rain in areas, but more will be needed over the next few months along with relatively warm temperatures. Respectively, contacts say October milk checks remained hearty. Processors say milk is widely available and growing. Class I demand is steady to lower. Retail ordering remains somewhat firm ahead of the holidays, but school district orders have lessened due to the upcoming winter break. Multiple cheesemaker contacts say they are full of self-sourced and/or contracted milk, therefore they are not seeking out extra spot loads. Milk loads for those cheesemakers still partaking from the spot market are mostly below Class III, but not as steeply discounted as they were during the holiday week. Cream is widely available throughout the region. Some butter plant managers passed along similar notes as cheesemakers; they say they cannot take on extra spot cream loads because they are at capacity. Cream usage related to ice cream production has slowed and is expected to continue to ease through the rest of the year. Both fluid milk and cream end users expect plentiful supplies to continue into the winter holidays.

Dairy farmers relay recent milk checks are a clear improvement when compared to previous years' amounts. They also continue to relay, despite some of them milking fewer cows, milk- and component-per-cow improvements year over year. There are still reports from processors and milk handlers that farms are continuing to close and/or shift into other arenas outside of dairy in the upper Midwest. According to contacts, closures are not all based on business, but younger generational pass-downs are not as common as they were. Milk output in the region has remained steady. That said, milk demand has edged lower in recent weeks. Plant updates/maintenance continue to be reported from Class II and Class III processors, while labor shortness remains prevalent. Cheesemakers reported spots from $3 under Class III to flat Class III. Some cheesemakers are starting to take on spot milk loads, as more spots have begun to be offered at lower prices. Spot cream loads are widely available for all uses. Ice cream manufacturers are being offered cream loads at multiples similar to Class IV pricing in recent weeks. Butter producers are also obtaining spots at multiples below the 1.20 mark and or slightly above. Contacts expect cream availability to remain intact into early 2023. Corn silage in the upper Midwest is reportedly in adequate supplies. Parts of the southern area of the region have received some much needed precipitation throughout the week, which is being welcomed by winter wheat farmers in areas from Kansas, Oklahoma and Texas. 

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during November was $3.7356, compared to $4.1936 in October.

Milk output in the region is continuing its seasonal incline. Farm milk yields, despite winter temperatures and precipitation moving into a majority of the Central region, including the southern states, are not expected to decline near term. Cooling temperatures and mostly positive reports regarding recent silage intakes/inventories are keeping those expectations either steady to upward in regards to milk production. Bottling plants are pulling some of that milk down, as Class I orders have been somewhat hearty in recent weeks, ahead of the holidays. Milk availability for other uses, though, is ample. Cheesemakers reported the strongest discounts of the season, as low as $6 under Class. Some cheese processors reported spot milk at Class, as well. Moving deeper into the winter holiday season, milk availability is not expected to decline for cheesemakers. Cream availability is mixed, but there were more notes from butter producers saying it was wholly plentiful at lower multiples. Still, depending on location and day of the week, some say multiples were steady, at best, with those of previous weeks. Ice cream production is slowing down at the end of the year and not expected to pick up over the next few weeks. Southern states within the region continue to receive some precipitation, which is being welcomed by farmer contacts. They suggest more will be needed to benefit wheat crops.

Trade Activity: Active

Central milk production is seasonally trending higher. Favorable milk prices, feed quality and weather have attributed to year over year milk growth across much of the region through the fall months. According to the November NASS Milk Production Report, in the Central region, only Illinois did not register a positive percentage change from November 2021. While farmers are producing more milk, processors are working hard to find ways to use it. Normally, the weeks surrounding the winter holidays can be challenging for milk handlers to find homes for milk and cream. This year, a major winter storm is exacerbating those challenges. Industry contacts report getting very strong milk orders at bottling plants early in the week as retail stores experienced a rush from shoppers stocking up before the storm. As the winter storm warnings were issued for later in the week, milk handlers have started to run into issues moving trucks around to assure pickups and deliveries. With some plants shut down for the holiday weekend, the number of places to move the milk is limited and some trucks could be idled, waiting to get unloaded. Calls between milk handlers and milk procurement workers are buzzing at a frenetic pace. In some cases, milk loads are discounted to $10 under Class III in order to get the milk placed. Processors expect the work to find homes for milk could continue through the weekend and into early next week. While industry contacts expect most of the milk will be able to find a home, they recognize the potential for some loads to be discarded because of delays or missed pickups. Cream volumes are also heavy. Similar to milk loads, some butter loads are getting discounted to get them placed and cream multiples have fallen off dramatically ahead of the storm and during the winter holidays.

Despite some short term snags due to the winter weather sweeping through the region last week, milk output is not expected to take much of a hit in the bigger picture outlook. Weather now, although cold in the upper Midwest and windy in the lower Central region, is not altogether unseasonable. In regards to last week's storms, milk handlers were met with some major complications in parts of the upper Midwest. They reported expectations of these complications ahead of the storms and still say they are playing catchup from all the backups and delays. In addition, plant laborers in some areas were asked not to report for scheduled shifts due to precautionary measures, so milk/cream trucks were held up at plants with shorthanded crews. Early last week, Class I orders were very strong as retailers were clearly aware that the customers were going to get ahead of the extreme weather conditions. Despite this, cheesemakers are still receiving strong discounts on milk. Spots ranged from $10 to $3 under Class III. Most of the reported prices were $6 to $10 under. Cheese plant managers say downtime is expected to be somewhat limited with the New Year holiday upcoming, as there is so much milk available to add to the vat. Cream availability has grown by leaps and bounds in the past two weeks. It is expected to remain somewhat available into the first weeks of 2023, as well. Butter churners are taking on a lot of the extra cream, but they are in similar situations to fluid milk processors already mentioned. Worker shortages are being noted regularly, particularly during the holiday season.

Recent weather has been more cooperative in regards to cow comfort levels in the entire region following the brief, but harsh, winter storm systems during the closing weeks of 2022. Retail pulls for the holidays/storms, and strengthened school orders in the past week, have kept bottlers busy. Cheesemakers are reporting strongly discounted prices in the wake of the holiday season. Near term expectations are uncertain on the longevity of $10 discounts on Class III milk loads. The generally mild upper Midwestern winter weather conditions have maintained a similar milk production outlook, pushing slightly higher week after week. Lower cream multiples were reported into butter plants this week in the southern states, where cream availability has increased.

Milk remains available for all uses in the Midwest and equally so in the southern states in the region. Mild recent temperatures have continued to bolster milk production, due to a relatively optimal cow comfort level for winter, up to now at least. Processors in the region say milk is available from both inside the region and from Western sources. Bottling pulls are steady, but clearly not putting a noticeable dent in overall availability. Spot milk prices remain as low as $10 under Class III. A winter storm in Kansas, Oklahoma and Texas has come and gone, but farmers/ranchers in those areas say another expected storm early next week is a growing concern. Snowfall is welcome and needed in large dry swaths covering the central and southern states. However, next week's forecasted storm is expected to bring ice. Current hay and feed inventories have farmers pushing more cows to auction, as livestock sales have been increasingly and notably busy in recent weeks.

Bottling orders are steady, but not putting much of a dent in overall supply for all processors in the Midwest. Class III spot milk prices continued to reach $10 under Class. A few processors suggest a locational variance, as some have said that spot milk loads are moving closer to $5 to $3 under Class in their respective areas, and that has been the case for weeks. Nonetheless, milk is widely available for end users throughout the region. Butter makers say cream has remained generally available, despite some increasing offer amounts. Butter producers who can opt for cream loads from Western states are also finding deals from that area, as well. Class II pulls are slowly edging higher, though. That said, the near term cream availability outlook is not expected to shift in a firming direction. Last week, farmers in the south central areas were concerned about ice storms, which have pounded areas of Texas and Oklahoma. Encumbrances to crop growth, milk production, processing and hauling are expected, but regional contacts say warmer temperatures, up to 60+ degrees expected over the weekend, are going to help.

Despite the winter weather, both freezing temperatures and precipitation, that has moved through the entire region in recent weeks, milk output is noted as steady. Bottlers are taking on a steady amount of milk loads, as demand is noted as stable, but not strong in any areas within the region. Cheesemakers are actively processing, as spot milk prices have reached $10 under Class III during week six. Week 50 of 2022 was the last time that -$10 milk/cwt milk prices were not the low end of the spot milk range, as $6 under Class III was the low end of the range that week. Nonetheless, there is plenty of milk for cheesemakers and processors. Cream availability remains somewhat steady for the most part. There were some cream multiples in recent weeks from the southern area of the region that were not relayed, so the bottom of the range moved higher, but butter plant managers and milk/cream handlers say multiples have generally been somewhat stable over the past few weeks. Cream end users in the upper Midwest reported finding some deals from the Western region, as well as locally sourced cream. High fertilizer costs and drought conditions have contacts in the South Central region suggesting hay shortages are expected to become a concern. Additionally, recent week's ice storms are another concern in regards to hay stores.  

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during January was $2.8713, compared to $3.2734 in December.

At report time, Midwestern states are being blanketed by sleet and heavy snow. Some processing plant contacts reported preemptively planning downtime due to the weather and potentially hazardous road conditions. This is unlikely to assist in already heavy milk supplies reported throughout the region, from north to south. Bottling plant orders are lighter due to upcoming school breaks. As northern states deal with blizzard conditions, crops in the central and southern states are growing in drought riddled areas. Conditions are variant not just from one area to another, but from one field to another.

Upper Midwestern farmers and milk truck drivers are or have until recently been working through late seasonal snowstorms. Nonetheless, milk output figures from farmers' monthly reports continue to show shifts higher in milk and milk components as spring flush nears. Milk availability remains a constant. Bottling orders are steady, despite dips here and there for spring breaks at some areas. The bottling sector, however, is not keeping cheesemakers from receiving spot milk offers well below the Class III mark. Spot prices this week were reported from $11 to $3 under Class. That said, for the first time in weeks, more prices are starting to edge near the higher end of the price range, as $4 and $5 under Class spot prices were more regularly reported than those at $10 and $11 under. Condensed skim notes continue to suggest widely available supplies. As plant downtime has kept some dryers from activity, contacts suggest condensed skim is expected to remain available for the near term. Cream availability is trending higher, as reported multiples were lower from week to week. Spring holiday demand upticks for processors, through last week, had spread the cream pool a little thinner throughout most of early March. Now, however, cream end users such as butter and ice cream processors have begun to step back from the spot market. In a rare occurrence, cream buyers in the region were offered cream loads from the Eastern region this week. Typically, cream is sourced from within the region or West of the Mississippi River. Farmers in the South Central region say they are somewhat regularly under red flag warnings, as windy and dry days continue to keep soil moisture quite limited. That said, winter wheat farmers say crops look fairly healthy this spring. There is a contrast in reports from South Central to Northern states. Kansas, Oklahoma and Texas are widely variant due to dryness, while Illinois and the Northern Plains states' wheat reports are steadier.

More late season snowfalls, over a foot in some areas, affected some upper Midwest and Northern Plains farms over the weekend. Despite this, farm milk output is only continuing to inch up week to week, as contacts say spring flush is looming. Spring flush's seasonal milk availability for end users did not need any seasonal demarcation, though, as milk has been widely accessible throughout the winter. Milk handlers in the areas effected by snow say they were working to shift milk and cream out of those specific areas. Bottlers continue to chip away at the overall milk pool, but cheesemakers in the upper Midwest continue to take on higher volumes of spot milk at prices as low as $11 under Class III. Condensed skim continues to be offered out at aggressive pricing for processors. Cream availability is mirroring that of milk's, as spring holidays approach. Ice cream production has quieted ahead of the holiday weekend. This has pushed more cream tanks into butter plants. Some butter contacts say plant downtime is not expected during the upcoming holiday, as churning rates remain near to or at capacity. Contacts in some parts of Texas say they have received some beneficial rain. That said, there a number of areas in the southern area of the region that are hot and dry, including other areas of the Lonestar State, Oklahoma and Kansas, which are warm, windy and dry. Winter wheat conditions are mixed, though, as some contacts in those regions relay somewhat good progress of winter wheat fields despite the dryness.

Some upper Midwestern contacts have suggested peak flush is upon the region. Ice cream manufacturing is still a little slower than some cream contacts expected at this point in the year, but seasonal upticks in production are, according to those same contacts, expected to increase later in the month ahead of seasonal demand increases. Lots of areas in the region are getting some rainfall. The Mississippi River, at or nearing its crest in areas of the Midwest, has some areas experiencing floods, while farmers in dryer areas of the same impacted states, specifically Iowa and Illinois, have already begun planting corn. Contacts in Wisconsin and Minnesota say planting preparations are underway, but soil temperatures remain too cold to plant corn there. 

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during April was $2.9629, compared to $2.9656 in March.

Butter makers have said they expect to run pretty heavy schedules ahead of the seasonally expected cream price increase. Corn and bean planting in the upper Midwest has been spurred on by a stretch of recently dry and sunny weather, as most reports are relaying corn planting being ahead of the recent years' average. Wheat harvest expectations in the central and southern states of the region are mixed, but some areas have received some much needed seasonal rainfall. That said, parts of Kansas, Texas and Oklahoma either received the rain too late for a hearty yield, or are still too dry to expect strong yields.

The talk of the industry is the far-reaching heatwave moving throughout the region, which has presented itself in most of the region moving into the final weeks of the summer. What Texas and its neighboring area have undergone for months is now being reported in the Upper Midwest and Northern Plains. South Dakota towns are reporting record highs, while schools in Iowa are instituting early dismissals. Missouri and Illinois farmers are facing strong heat with little to no precipitation expected in the near-term. All said, triple digit temperatures and, in some areas, high humidity are keeping milk and component production in check. Bottling orders have increased throughout the region. This has kept the fluid milk market somewhat snug, but the cream spinoff from bottling low-fat milk has bolstered cream availability for butter makers and other cream end users. Most cheesemaker contacts say milk offers have dwindled in recent weeks. Due to plant downtime, some say there was pressure on the increasing prices, but spots were reported from Class III to $.50 over Class. As was mentioned, cream availability has grown in the region. Butter makers reported having options in the same price range from both the Western region and local sources. Ice cream manufacturing slowdowns and the aforementioned spinoff from Class I facilities have brought cream down to earth from recent multiples at or near 1.50. Condensed skim supplies vary by area, but generally they are reported as tight to tightening. Preparations to find milk/cream destinations ahead of the Labor Day holiday weekend are already underway according to handlers in the region.

Milk and its components are increasing gradually at the farm level. Seasonal improvements have been reported, but more strongly in the southern states of the Central region. Improvements are relative, because Texas and its neighboring states had a notably harsh summer. Regular temperatures in the triple digits, along with very little rain, strained dairy herds in the area. Temperatures are still warm in that area, but nights and mornings have cooled down considerably. In the Upper Midwest, cow comfort has been less of a concern, but cooperative contacts still say the summer saw a notable drop-off of dairy farm operations, particularly herds with around or under 200 head. Class I intakes continue to steadily keep milk availability in check for processing. That said, cheesemakers reported spot prices at $.25- to $2-over Class III. They say despite some plants allotting extra downtime in recent and upcoming weeks, spot milk remains somewhat snug. Last year, during week 40, spot milk prices ranged from $2-under to $1-over Class. Condensed skim availability continues to seasonally increase. Handlers say customers are less willing to pay higher values. Cream availability remains tight in the region. Butter makers say they are bidding for spot cream loads, but finding loads below the 1.30 multiple mark in the region has become less common. Class III manufacturers are busier in recent weeks, fulfilling seasonal retail needs. Early October has brought on corn harvest in earnest in the Corn Belt. Warmer and dryer than average seasonal weather has given some farmers the incentive to get in their fields in the Midwest, while rains have delayed others. 

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during September was $3.6792, compared to $3.7712 in August.

As has been reported, Midwestern dairy farm closures trended higher than in previous years this summer. Generational dairy farms are shuttering or moving into other sectors of the agriculture industry. As seasonal milk availability would typically begin to edge nearer to fulfilling processing capacities, it is not. Some contacts are beginning to question if the impact of those farm closures is playing a part, or if it is simply a matter of milk supply and demand, which has been lopsided in the region after a notable amount of milk was dispersed from north to south (including southwest/southeast) in the latter half of the summer. Class I intakes are steady. Class III spot milk prices are still over market. Last year in week 41, cheesemakers were paying $1 under to $.25 over Class III. Some contacts say cream has loosened when compared to previous weeks. They say this week may have been the dividing line from seasonally tight to seasonally available markets. Early week cream multiples were in the 1.30s, mid- to late-week trades were in the 1.20s. Other contacts say cream markets, and end users, are justly uncertain because of the abnormal butter market boom. Contacts say there were fewer bulls in Pamplona in July than in the U.S. butter market in October. Some butter makers in the region continue to say spot cream remains too firm for their access. Farmers in the Midwest have combines in the fields. Reports say despite the limited rain and, in some cases, paltry yield predictions this growing season, some farmers are passing along more positive-than-expected early results.

Contacts have relayed some updates from their September monthly reports and, as was expected, component levels have begun to show a tangible increase month to month in the Midwest. Cream end users are benefitting from these component bumps, although fluid milk processors have yet to see much change in regard to pricing. Class I demand is steady to slower this report week. There are some slight variances in states where schools are approaching fall breaks. Cheesemakers reported spot milk prices as low as Class III for the first time since late August. That said, spot prices as high as $2-over Class were also reported this week. Some cheesemakers continue to say offers are somewhat quiet. Class II and Class III cream usage has shifted lower, as ice cream, cream cheese, and dip/whip manufacturing has begun to slow down. Butter churning is picking up. Butter makers say spot cream multiples in the low 1.20s are easier to justify, fiscally speaking, to add to their churning schedules. Corn harvesting, for silage, is nearly completed in most areas of the region, while harvest for grain is roughly halfway through. Areas of the Corn Belt have had some relatively heavy rain this week, but farmers are expected to be able to re-enter fields currently in a number of those same areas. Corn and bean harvests, though, are running at or near previous years' averages throughout the Central region.

Fluid milk and cream markets typically trend in a somewhat uniform direction, naturally. Recent weeks have eschewed the commonalities, though. Fluid milk remains noticeably snug for this time of year. Class I orders, cheesemaking, and other processing needs have kept milk availability somewhat tight in the region. The summer that brought along a scarcity of milk availability due to extreme temperatures and climactic conditions in much of the country, but particularly the southern swath, has kept a lengthier delay on replenishing many pipelines. Despite increases in feed availability and much milder fall weather, spot milk prices remain above Class III moving into the final two months of the year. Cheesemakers in the upper Midwest continue to say they are not receiving increased offerings from spot milk suppliers. Those that are receiving offers say they have yet to move much lower in price. This week's reported spot milk range was $.25 to $2.00 over Class III. Last year, during week 43, spot prices ranged from $1 under to $.50 over Class. On the cream side, those markets have loosened noticeably over the past few weeks. Butter plant contacts say they are churning at more than double the amount they were earlier in the same month. Cream multiples in the fiscal comfort zone of butter makers, low to mid 1.20s, are favoring those increases in churning. Cream usage from Class II and III manufacturers has markedly slowed in the past month, particularly for Class III. Ice cream manufacturing had a quieter late summer, so those slowdowns were noted earlier in the year than usual. Small grain farmers in the southern states have planted crops and are receiving some beneficial rainfall prior to the first frost. They are hopeful the current precipitation does not morph from helpful to excessive, as forecasts are showing a number of rainy days ahead. Upper Midwestern farmers have received a lot of rain. Contacts say they are hopeful for some solidifying frosts to get machinery back into the fields.

Last year, contacts reported spot milk prices from $3-under to flat Class the week after Thanksgiving. By the end of December 2022, $10-under Class prices became a weekly fixture in the report. At the moment, milk and cream markets are more balanced than they were this time last year when the proverbial floodgates opened. Cream, like fluid milk, was widely available with the plant downtime last week. Spots were reported at flat 1.00 multiple. Butter makers say that was short-lived though, as they reported spot offers and trades in the 1.16+ realm, up to 1.20+. Class II and III demand needs are, at least near-term, picking up some from pre-Thanksgiving weeks. Feed quality/quantity is reportedly top-tier right now, and with fewer cows/herds across the region, values-per-cow are reportedly keeping dairy operators content. 

Price Range - Class III Milk; $/CWT; Spot Basis:    .00 - 1.00 
Trade Activity: Slow

In a typical week, cream and fluid markets generally parallel one another. Cream availability increases, as does fluid milk availability, or the opposite. Recent trends, though, have eschewed the norm. Cream availability slightly ticked lower, as multiples increased. During the same week, fluid milk volumes for spot market usage increased, evidenced by decreasing prices at the cheese plant intake level. Class I milk intakes were steady. Despite school breaks upcoming, which are expected and planned for ahead of time, bottlers' foci have been set on retail bottling, which has kept a good amount of milk from processing end uses. That said, cheesemakers reported more under-Class pricing this week than during the Thanksgiving holiday week. Spot milk prices were reported as low as $4-under Class III. Still, there were some early week spot prices reported at the $1-over Class price. Milk output, and particularly component levels have benefited during the generally mild fall. Despite there being snow on the ground in areas of the Upper Midwest at report time, 50 degree temperatures this coming weekend are likely to change the wintry landscape. Also, the number of herds continues to shrink in the Upper Midwest due to strong beef markets and volatile farmgate milk check amounts. All said, there are more questions than answers when it comes to what early 2024 will bring regarding fluid milk output and availability. Condensed skim availability has been somewhat open. Handlers, though, say that recent demand from processors is strengthening. Cream is not tight, but it is continuing to slowly firm following the holiday-related onslaught of availability. Butter maker contacts are continuing to micro-fix, as well as churn, and have been hesitant to bid on "too much" cream. They do say, however, that as the end-of-year holidays approach, they expect cream orders and, naturally, churning rates to push higher as they get ahead of spring holiday demand-required inventories. Cream handlers report that butter and cream cheese end users are somewhat active on the spot market, while ice cream manufacturing is the sole holdout when it comes to busier activity. 

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during November was $3.6111, compared to $4.4488 in October.

Fluid milk growth continues to be reported in the region. Late fall weather has been somewhat mild, although wetter, in the Upper Midwest. Feed quality/quantities have reportedly been seasonally hearty. Steady improvements in milkfat contents continue to be reported by farmers sharing information from their monthly reports. Class I intakes have slowed down. Retailers' holiday orders have been fulfilled by this week, while food service orders have lagged due to mid-year vacations at school districts. More milk is making its way into other channels, namely Class III plants. Cheesemakers reported spot prices as low as $5-under Class, while some early week trades remained in last week's range at $.25-over Class III. Milk handlers say the next two weeks will be somewhat hectic in trying to secure destinations with consecutive Monday holidays ahead. Cream handlers are in the same boat. They say butter churning is busier, as butter plant staff are taking advantage of holiday level cream values, while adding to their spring-demand cold storage capacities. All said, milk and cream are more in line this week, as both are widely available.

NOTICE: The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) is seeking nominations from fluid milk processors and other interested parties for candidates to serve on the National Fluid Milk Processor Promotion Board. The deadline for nominations is Jan. 12, 2024. USDA will accept nominations for board representation in six geographic regions and two at-large positions. Nominees for the regional positions and one of the at-large positions must be active owners or employees of a fluid milk processor. The other at-large position may be an active owner or employee of a fluid milk processor or a member of the general public. To nominate individuals, a copy of the nomination form and a signed background form must be submitted for each nominee by Jan. 12, 2024. Nomination forms and information are available on the Agricultural Marketing Service (AMS) Fluid Milk Processor Promotion Board webpage. For more information, please review the Notice to Trade at: https://www.ams.usda.gov/content/usda-seeks-nominees-national-fluid-milk-processor-promotion-board-4.

Fluid milk and cream handlers are under strong pressure to find destinations for their trucks. Holiday production schedules throughout the Classes have been light, and in some cases lighter than expected. Some manufacturing plant managers are allotting four-day weekends. Bottling orders shifted lower earlier than some expected last week, and spot milk prices and cream multiples this week are evidence of an abundance of milk and plant schedule downtime. Cheesemakers are taking on spot milk loads as low as $8-under Class III and the highest reported price was $2-under Class. Last year's prices during week 52 ranged from $10- to $3-under Class. Class II and III manufacturers are quiet for the season. Butter makers in the Midwest reportedly have cream trucks lined up at the plants, as spot cream multiples ranged as low as .5, pushing prices lower. Early-week spot cream multiples were at and around 1.00.

Midwestern and Northern Plains contacts say the recently mild winter weather has created hearty milk and component output at the farm level. Adequate feedstuffs, moderate precipitation and generally comfortable weather have combined to bring on bolstered monthly reports from dairy farms, particularly regarding milkfat component levels. Winter, though, is about to make itself known. Forecasted temperatures over the coming weekend and into next week have some negative signs attached in the Dakotas, and single-digit lows in parts of the Upper Midwest and into Iowa and Kansas. Bottling orders have grown. Milk handlers note that the first week of the year is typically busier for Class I orders, while the second week tends to slump. Spot milk remains widely available, but last year's stretch of $10-under Class III spot milk prices in the Midwest is not expected to repeat itself. Spots were reported as low as $8-under Class this week, but by midweek prices began to stabilize and offers moved nearer to Class. Cream multiples/prices rebounded in quick fashion following the ample availability throughout the holiday season. Class II and III manufacturing remains quiet, according to contacts, while churners have been running hard over the past three weeks. Condensed skim availability is somewhat open, but handlers say they are finding homes for skim solids, albeit with some longer-than-typical hauling routes.    

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during December was $2.9879, compared to $3.6111 in November. The 2023 annual average was $3.3010.

Winter weather is playing a bigger role this week in fluid milk and cream markets when it comes to production and logistics. Some areas have already faced blizzard-like weather, as Midwest and Plains states are set to face at least one winter storm since the beginning of the week. Most states in the region will have dealt with severe temperatures and wintry precipitation by the end of the weekend, though. Class I ordering, early in the week, was busier than a normal second week of the year, as bottlers are getting ahead of upcoming retail needs. Cheesemakers reported spot milk prices from $7- to $.50-under Class III. During week two of 2023, spot prices were $10- to $3-under Class. Contacts are attributing current spot milk availability to plant downtime more than milk output growth, as a number of cheese plant managers/contacts relayed down-days this week. Cream handlers say ice cream manufacturing remains sluggish. Churns, though, are running actively, with the exception of weather-related downtime. Butter makers say they are turning away cream offers, as they are currently pushing capacity.

Milk and cream availability for processing continue to perplex contacts in the region. After the typical holiday abundance late last year, trends were generally following their regular pattern; availability was slowly tightening up following seasonal milk output increases, as plant activity was returning to more normal weekly routines. The winter blast that began last week in parts of the region, and by the end of the weekend, had affected at least portions in every state in the Central region put a kibosh on perceived normalcy in the fluid milk and cream markets. Plant contacts in a variety of dairy manufacturing capacities reported issues due to the cold and precipitation that slammed the middle of the country last week. From trucks being stuck roadside to frozen equipment, dairy facility employees tackled myriad hardships over the past 10 days. Milk and cream handlers both have had to reroute trucks and move loads to alternate destinations. Bottlers' intakes were held up during the arctic wave, but intakes are expected to pick up posthaste to replenish some retail and food service (namely school district) pipelines. Spot milk prices for cheese processing held in the same range as last week. There were more trades reported this week than any week since before the holidays. Last year, during week three, the spot milk price range was $10- to $6-under Class III. Contacts in the region do not expect a repeat of last year's $10-under Class streak, or anything in that ballpark. That said, there are currently more questions than answers when it comes to near-term milk availability. Cream availability continues to be somewhere between abundant and copious. Class III processors are clearing more cream than expected. Butter makers say they are taking on hearty cream volumes and still turning down offers. Contacts in the southern states reported multiples as low as .50, while Midwestern cream values are holding in a similar pattern to last week between 1.00 and 1.20, for the most part. Contacts concur that ice cream manufacturing remains notably slow.

In what was a recurring theme through the latter weeks of 2023, milk and cream availability have reportedly diverged this week. Fluid milk availability is starting to show signs of a rebound from the holiday season. Cream markets, on the other hand, have held in a similar holiday-like pattern. Despite more inclement weather early in the week, namely in the south/central states where farmers and haulers contended with more icy conditions, Class I interests returning to the fray have put some upward pricing pressure on spot milk for other Class end users. Cheese processing is also more consistent across the region this week, on the whole. Spot milk prices were reported from $1-under Class to $1-over Class III. During week four of 2023, spot milk prices ranged from $10- to $4-under Class III. Cheesemakers relay milk suppliers' offers have quieted down this week. Cream availability, despite multiples moving higher in most facets, remains abundant. Clearly, Class II manufacturing sluggishness continues to put pressure on cream handling contacts to find destinations for their volumes. Routing has been a concern, due to both the weather and limited end user demand. Butter churning is busy in the region, but butter plant managers relay turning away cream load spot offers at below a 1.00 multiple in some cases. Condensed skim availability has steadied in recent weeks, according to contacts. Beyond the near term, contacts have shared concerns about tightening markets across the board. Upper Midwestern dairy farmers continue to exit the industry, as new processing plants throughout the region are set to start operations during this year. Moving further into 2024, milk and milk component availability, despite the aforementioned surplus over recent weeks, has some contacts concerned. There are expectations of stronger processing pulls, while dairy farm output questions continue to loom.

Now, though, the cream situation is noted as loose.

There is a general sentiment among Central region contacts that the 2024 milk output picture will not be in line with that of 2023. The continuation/consistency of farm closures in the region, bolstered beef markets, and feed quality/availability are just some of the factors contacts point to when laying out their expectations. Contacts are curious as to what flush will look like in upcoming weeks and how the fluid milk/cream markets will respond when more processing plants are online, in the region, later this year. Currently, milk output has leveled off. Improved weather conditions, particularly in regard to cow comfort, have been beneficial for milk production conditions. That said, processors continue to say milk solids are not where they were just a month ago. Bottling pulls remain steady, but some contacts note that milk is being pulled in different directions and out of the region altogether in some cases. Cheesemakers reported no notable changes in milk availability after last week's bump higher on the reported spot milk price range. Regionally, cheesemakers are steadily active. Spot milk prices ranged from Class III to $1-over Class. Last year, during week six, the price range was $10- to $4-under Class III. Cream does remain available, though. Spots, again, were reported below market, and butter churns continue to be the primary destination for cream tankers. That said, for the first time in weeks, cream cheese processing has started to increase according to some contacts. Class II manufacturing demand for cream remains sidelined. Some have expectations that the nearing spring season will increase ice cream processors' appetites for cream, but they do not expect cream availability to tighten up until the end of February/early March. Condensed skim availability continues to slowly tighten up. Skim handlers say processing is not at capacity. They relay that if skim solids remain as tight as they are currently, prices could push higher when processing rates increase.  

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during January was $3.0279, compared to $2.9879 in December.

The much awaited first NASS Milk Production report of 2024 noted a 0.9 percent downtrend for milk for January when compared to January 2023 (in the 24 selected states.) Notable shifts in the Central region were states such as South Dakota, which showed a 10.8 percent gain, and Texas' milk production, which slid 3.9 percent lower. Warmer and milder winter weather in the region has kept week to week expectations somewhat steady. Contacts are raising questions about what this year's flush will bring. Most contacts expect lighter milk supplies compared to last year. Higher cull rates, closing dairy farms and reverberation from lower milk prices from last year are all catalysts for the expected downticks. Class I demand has held somewhat steady in recent weeks. Spring break preparations in the educational sector will slow down near-term bottling pulls temporarily. Cheesemakers say spot milk load offers have quieted. There were some spot milk trades below the reported range, which is $.50-under to $.50-over Class III, but they were situational. Most trading is falling at or just around Class. Last year, during week eight, spot market prices were reported from $10- to $2.50-under Class III. Condensed skim availability is unchanged. Some current and upcoming plant downtime has milk handlers expecting growths in availability in coming weeks. Cream remains in a similar status as it has throughout much of 2024. Spot loads of cream are still being reported at market. Ice cream manufacturing has yet to gain much traction. Butter makers had expectations that cream would be widely available throughout February and they were correct. Some expect somewhat open availability moving into March.

Dairy Market News colleagues and contacts have shared their concerns about the wildfires in the panhandles of Texas and Oklahoma. Reports suggest over one million acres have now burned, and containment of some of the blazes is minimal as of Thursday morning. Contacts are hopeful snowfall moving in from New Mexico will assist firefighters' efforts. The area in jeopardy is an agricultural and livestock foothold, and dairy operations have notably grown there in recent years. 

Milk production upticks in the Midwest are being more regularly reported. Weather, despite being somewhat erratic this week with warm 60 and 70 degree temperatures one day and sub-freezing temperatures the next, has generally been somewhat cow-comfortable in the later winter weeks. Contacts relay some Upper Midwestern corn farmers have begun planting in small sections well-ahead of schedule due to mild winter weather. As spring approaches, milkfat content, particularly, has been notably strong. Class I demand is ticking lower week-over-week, and some contacts relay year-over-year numbers are also lower. Spring break related downticks in demand ahead are likely to push more milk into other processing channels. This week, though, spot milk prices into Class III plants were higher. Reported spot prices ranged from $0.25- to $2.50-over Class III. Last year, during week nine, spot prices ranged from $10- to $2.50-under Class III. Condensed skim availability is growing. With seasonal upticks in milk production and plant downtime being regularly reported, condensed skim demand is steady, but availability has increased. Cream is also widely available. This week marks another week of flat-market multiples being reported in the region. That said, a number of market contacts say milkfat demand is getting a seasonal bump moving into March. They expect cream to be somewhat available next month, but not at the abundant levels it has been so far this year. Class III and IV manufacturers are steady to very active, while Class II has been atypically quiet moving into the spring season.

Milk demand from the Class I sector is sluggish. School district spring breaks are what most contacts suggest is the primary catalyst for buying slowdowns at the bottling level. Simultaneously, milk output is moving higher throughout most of the region. State agencies and the USDA have acknowledged what was recently coined a "mystery illness" in dairy herds in the Texas Panhandle and Kansas. The presence of diseased migratory birds linked to sickness among a percentage of cows in the same vicinity have led veterinarian authorities to identify Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) as the illness affecting some dairy animals. Officials relay no threat to the public, nor do they expect milk supply shortages as a result. Adding to that sentiment, contacts in Texas and the southern/central U.S. states suggest milk output is nearing peak spring flush levels. Texas contacts also report corn growth/planting in their areas is near to or meeting annual expectations. Upper Midwestern processors relay strong component levels have provided plenty of milk/milk ingredients for all end uses. Cheesemakers reported spot milk prices below previous weeks' figures. Spot milk load prices ranged from $6- to $0.50-under Class III. Some say similar, or even lower, prices are expected ahead of the spring holiday weekend. Cream availability has grown in the region. Cream demand from Class III plant operators remains somewhat hearty. Butter plant managers relay widely available cream volumes, as some are already booking cream through April at what they consider a value.

The early spring has brought a wide variety of weather conditions to the Central region. In the Upper Midwest and Northern Plains, snow is falling at report time as farmers and truckers in that area were already contending with snow cover from earlier in the week. In the southern states, early-week storms more typically associated with the spring season brought farmers and crops hail, tornadic activity, and rainfall. The Center for Disease Control (CDC) reported a dairy worker in Texas tested positive for the Highly Pathogenic Avian Flu (HPAI), but the CDC considers the threat to the public to be low. Farms in three Central states (Michigan, Texas and Kansas) have had positive HPAI tests in dairy cows. Producers are working closely with veterinary experts and governing agencies to increase biosecurity measures and limit the transmission of HPAI. Contacts have mixed views regarding the eventual effects of HPAI on dairy markets, but a number say it should not impact, greatly, overall milk output or availability. Class I demand is a bit heartier this week, as school terms resume following spring break in many areas of the region. Cheesemakers say more plants have resumed their regular schedules, which has kept spot milk availability a little more balanced for Class III production. Spot milk prices reported from cheesemakers ranged from $5-under to $1.50-under Class III, moving higher at the bottom of the range when compared to last week. Last year, during week 14, spot milk prices ranged from $11- to $3-under Class. Cream availability remains ample, but sub-1.00 multiples from last week were no longer reported. Butter makers say they have April cream needs booked, as regional Class IV multiples are falling, for the most part, in and around the 1.15 mark. Cream handlers say demand is somewhat active. They say demand firmed more quickly than expected following the holiday weekend, particularly from cream cheese and ice cream manufacturers.

Weekly upticks in milk output are being reported at the farm level, but processors say component levels are ahead of expectations. Those who are clearing spot milk are paying from $2- to $1-under Class III. Class I intakes remain steady, but bottling plant activity is expected to slow down notably in the near-term, as school districts in the southern states of the region are, in some cases, down to their penultimate weeks of the schoolyear. Cream remains widely available for all end users. Ice cream production is ticking higher, but butter makers say May availability is similar to April's, and there is no shortage of offers on the board for spot cream loads.

The seasonal distinctions of week-over-week milk output are beginning to become more pronounced between the South Central states and the Midwest/Upper Midwest. With warmer days in the southern states and a stronger pull from processors, contacts in that area of the Central region say spot milk availability is moving from seasonally flush to balanced even as bottling plant orders have lightened with the culmination of the schoolyear in the area. Class III spot milk prices ranged from $4-under to $1-over Class III in the Upper Midwest. The aforementioned processing plant downtime is the cause of prices moving lower again this week. Cream remains available for all Class uses, but prices are beginning to tick higher. Ice cream manufacturing is shifting up seasonally, but cream handlers suggest Class III end users are a greater impact on decreasing the overall cream pool moving into mid-May. Farmers in parts of the southern states in the region are preparing for the winter wheat harvest, but stormy/wet conditions are keeping combines out of the fields at report time. Moving north, into the Midwest/Upper Midwest, corn planting and hay harvests are facing some of the same conditions.

Storms throughout the Central U.S. are affecting farmers and plant activities from north to south. Tornadic activity and strong winds/heavy rain have cut power at processing plants in multiple states. This has kept milk handlers very busy from last weekend through midweek. They say trying to find new homes for milk trucks has been increasingly difficult. Milk output continues to stabilize after what some contacts are labeling a muted flush season in the Upper Midwest. Class I intakes are trending lower as school districts are either wrapping up or are already on summer break. Cheesemakers reported spot milk loads from $6- to $.50-under Class. The combination of storms/inclement weather and the impending holiday weekend are adding to spot milk availability regionally. Contacts do not expect milk to remain this available for long, though. A number of cheesemakers said they were not looking for extra milk this week, but prices were too enticing to overlook. Cream multiples held in a similar pattern this week, despite the Memorial Day weekend. That said, contacts do expect some late-week/weekend multiples to shift closer to, or to, flat market rates. At report time, cream prices are somewhat in line with recent weeks. Cream contacts say ice cream demand continues to tick higher week-over-week. Expectations vary from contact to contact regarding upcoming cream availability. Some expect somewhat robust volumes to continue into July, while others think tighter seasonal trends will come sooner. Fieldwork continues to be delayed due to the aforementioned weather. Wheat harvest in the southern states is expected to begin in the next few weeks, but wetness throughout the region is adding to potential delays. Planting opportunities in the Midwest and Northern Plains are in a similar situation.

Milk production has eased in the region. Farmers continued to face spring storms over the weekend and early into the week. Upper Midwest contacts say rain, which was needed, has come. Despite relative milk production and availability being somewhat stable this year so far, particularly when compared to the heavy milk pool of 2023, fluid milk end users are saying that components have been strong. This week, for the first time, some of those same contacts are seeing a contraction in component levels. Right now, though, there is plenty of milk throughout the region for end users. The Monday holiday, along with a number of plants being down due to storm-related power outages, kept milk and cream handlers very busy over the weekend. Additionally, school milk orders have begun to decline, with sharper declines noted in the southern states. Cheesemakers say that their milk needs are being met, to put it lightly. A number have said they are turning away spot milk offers at below-Class prices, as they are full and running active schedules. Reported spot milk prices ranged from $6- to $.50-under Class III. Cream availability held steadier over the holiday week than some contacts expected. Butter makers say they are still clearing cream loads at similar multiples to those in April and throughout May. Class III cream end users are still somewhat active on the market, and ice cream manufacturing is seasonally picking up. Regarding cream and milk availability, in general, the post-holiday and storm-affected plant downtime has created a current surplus. That said, a number of contacts have questions regarding upcoming availability. Last summer, milk was spread somewhat thin with more tankers moving from the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic states into the southern states. That same situation is expected in the summer of 2024, but overall milk access throughout much of the current year has been quite a bit lighter than it was during the first half of 2023. With a growing amount of extenuating circumstances that lead contacts and analysts to expect lighter milk output mid-term, there are clearly more questions than answers as to what milk and dairy markets will look like in the second half of 2024. Regarding the crop outlook, as most of the region continues to grapple with wet weather, among other climactic challenges, planters, sprayers and harvesters are facing some delays. Still, farmers in some areas have cut first crop hay in the Midwest while others are waiting for dryer fields.

***Update: Corrected Class III Milk Price***
Cow comfort in the Central region is being tested this week by varying conditions. In the south, high temperatures and heavy rainfall are putting a seasonal crimp on output. Precipitation is also playing a role in the Upper Midwest, but temperatures have yet to take hold at summer-like levels for the most part in the northern states of the region. Component level shifts have been noted for several weeks. Some contacts say they have seen slightly lower components, while others have said decreases are more noticeable. Bottling orders have seasonally quieted throughout the region. That said, despite the previous holiday week's milk availability boost, Class III spot milk prices moved higher this week on the bottom of the range. Reported spot milk prices ranged from $2- to $1-under Class III. Condensed skim availability has mirrored that of spot milk. Skim handlers relay that as demand has pushed higher, availability is moving lower. All that said, cream markets are undergoing the most noteworthy seasonal shift this week. Active ice cream manufacturing, along with steady Class III usage, has butter makers saying the cream availability narrative has shifted toward the tighter end of the spectrum. Expectations are that butter churning will lighten in the near-term. Regarding the aforementioned rainfall throughout the region, crop farmers continue to face delays and hurdles. Limitations are being reported throughout the region based on wet and muddy conditions, while farmers from Texas up through the Dakotas wait for dryer days ahead. 

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during May was $3.7333, compared to $3.5749 in April.

Triple-digit temperatures in the southern states within the region are beginning to set in at more regular pace. Recently wet weather has led southern states' contacts to also point out high humidity levels. None of which bode well for ideal cow comfort standards. Even in the Upper Midwest, where temperatures remain more in line with favorable cow comfort, component shifts downward continue to be reported by processors. Class I is in the early stages of its seasonal lull. Retail and some modified school district needs (namely those for summer and school meal programs) continue to keep some milk loads moving into bottling plants, but the majority of milk is moving into other channels. Cheesemakers reported prices in the same range as last week. There are a number of cheesemakers saying they are more willing to take on spot milk, particularly at sub-Class prices. This week's range of $2- to $1-under Class III shows the drastic difference in milk availability for cheesemakers from year to year. During week 24 of 2023, the spot milk price range was $12- to $3.50-under Class. Some cheesemakers expect a steady to balanced milk supply until the July 4th holiday, but after that most expect continued firmness. Cream availability has continued to move seasonally lower. Butter makers in the region say cream intakes are lower week to week, and like their cheesemaking counterparts, expect seasonal tightness to continue to limit production. Additionally, ice cream manufacturers are pulling more from the overall cream pool in the region. All said, there are significant shifts taking place with cream end users in the region. Butter plant managers expect to see micro-fixing schedules pick up, as churning ebbs, and as Class II and Class III manufacturers have begun to chip away at a seasonally declining cream shed. Despite the aforementioned humidity, wet weather has begun to move away from the south-Central area of the region, while Upper Midwestern farmers are still contending with ongoing rain and wet fields.

Central fluid milk output continues to see seasonal decreases across the region. As summer looms, summer-like temperatures have begun to affect more areas of the region, but particularly the southern area. Regular rainfall in the Midwest has contacts and farmers there suggesting humidity is playing a part in creating less-than-ideal cow comfortability. Additionally, there were some anecdotal reports of corn being in noticeably wet ground. Some, though, say offers have quieted in the past few weeks, and they are awaiting the Independence Day week to look to secure extra loads. Cream availability continues to tighten. Butter makers say they are still churning at somewhat regular intervals, but few expect cream volumes to hold below the mid-1.20 mark for multiples for much longer. Ice cream manufacturing and Class III cream usage continues to pull tightening cream supplies away from butter churning.

In recent weeks, there are more reports of potential dairy farm expansions in the Central region. Farmers are looking to grow their herds/operations, primarily via internal husbandry, as heifer prices are notably bullish. The combination of affordable/available feed and stronger farmgate milk checks has been a formula for potential augmentation. Milk output, though, is seasonally stable. Component levels are strong, and contacts are now questioning whether protein/fat content in milk has reached its zenith. Class I milk intakes are stable. Processors continue to report two factors that, during a typical timeframe, would seem incongruent: plant managers have regularly reported maintenance and scheduled downtime, but spot milk prices are remaining steady or increasing. Cheesemakers and milk vendors are currently working through 2025 contractual negotiations, with expectations of, or already confirmed, price increases for milk loads. Dryer weather in southern and central areas of the region have given less than robust notes on corn yields there, but farmers in the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest are experiencing or expecting to yield a healthier bounty this harvest season. Condensed skim availability has grown. Skim handlers say stocks are not burdensome, as of yet, but the aforementioned plant downtime has put some pressure on them to rehome some extra volumes. Cream availability remains ample. Butter makers have suggested they are booking fall/winter holiday cream at 1.00 multiples, while current multiples continue to be reported below to just above 1.20 for butter churners. Cream cheese manufacturers are beginning to pick up the pace, though, as cream handlers have said Class III cream end usage could, at least slightly, tighten up what has been a seasonally available cream market.      

The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during September was $4.0877, compared to $4.3136 in August.

The seasonal and gradual growth in milk output has begun, in earnest. Contacts say milk components are, in some cases, at historic levels in the Upper Midwest, most notably milkfat levels. Processors have shared in that sentiment. The year, so far, has been notably different in certain aspects regarding fluid milk and cream markets in the region. As was mentioned, component levels have continued to improve/increase month after month (and year after year.) Despite somewhat heavy cream availability, milk availability in the region has been relatively tight. Class I pulls have been noticeably strong later in the calendar year than a number of processors say they expected. Another somewhat anomalous occurrence took place this week. After last week's drops on both cream multiples and Class III spot milk prices, both markets rebounded the week ahead of Thanksgiving. Contacts suggest the upcoming weekend could bring some of those lower multiples/prices, but at report time they are not accounted for. Some cheesemakers in the Midwest say they are seeking out spot milk for next week and to their surprise, they have yet to lock down extra milk. Others, though, say milk is generally in balance and they are securing some extra loads for the holiday weekend. Spot milk prices last year during week 47, which was the week of Thanksgiving, were $2.50-under to $.25-over Class III. Cream demand picked up this week, both from Class II and Class III processors. Butter makers say they are still turning away extra spot loads, but cream availability shored up on the low end of the multiple range. Despite turning extra cream down, churning is active in the region. Butter makers and other cream end users are looking ahead to some downtime next week. That said, cream suppliers have suggested Thanksgiving Day is the only day next week that cream has become difficult to situate. As the corn harvest wraps up ahead of the average schedule, snow and cold temperatures are expected in parts of the Midwest.

Winterlike days are dwindling down, while warm days are beginning to become more regular for dairy farmers in the region, some of whom say thawing has created an extra muddy milking parlor in recent weeks. Despite the mud, cows are putting out more milk on a weekly basis. Milk component levels continued to slip, though. Farmers and processors say the milkfat numbers are quicker to decline than protein, as silage begins to show its age, but the decreases are minimal week to week. Class I demand is quiet. As school districts are moving into spring break season, there were fewer milk loads being pulled into Class I facilities again this week. Cheesemakers continue to suggest that despite lighter Class I activity, milk availability is generally in balance from one week to the next. Spot prices ranged from $2.50-under Class III to $.30-over Class. Again this week, some cheesemakers were on reduced production schedules. Cream availability continued its slow march to tighter seasonal availability. Multiples on the low end pushed up again this week, while Class II and Class III multiples held in a similar range to last week. Despite upticks in cream values, cream is far from tight and widely available for all uses. Butter churning remains very busy, as Class IV processors continue to add to upcoming seasonal pushes. Cream cheese and ice cream manufacturers have yet to move into a higher gear, but cream suppliers are expecting busier weeks to come as the spring holidays approach.  NOTE: Due to changes in the dairy industry, Dairy Market News will be realigning coverage of weekly cream price information. During the remainder of March 2025, Reports 11 - 14, Dairy Market News Central fluid report will include entries for Midwest cream prices. Beginning with Report 15, only Central cream prices will be included.

Wednesday, the last official day of winter, was a reminder that spring had not officially begun in the Central region. Conditions were variant from the south to the north; rain, ice, snow and strong winds were reported throughout the Central states. Despite farmers getting in fields in the Upper Midwest earlier this week/month, there are some near-term delays expected due to those wintery conditions. Despite that, milk production continues to increase, while milkfat components have continued to shift lower, slowly, but they remain above typical levels for late March. Class I movements have picked up some due to schools returning from spring break downtime. Cheesemakers were more active on the spot milk market on the whole, although some said they were not looking to add to current production schedules. Spot milk prices remain in a similar range to previous weeks, but upcoming downtime, alongside spring flush levels of milk availability, has some milk handlers preparing for supply growth. Cream markets continued in a similar trend to previous weeks. Cream remains widely available, but suppliers continue to say finding destinations is much easier now than it was two to three weeks ago. Upticks in ice cream and Class III manufacturing have assisted them in their efforts. Still, the cream dynamic has shifted when compared to year-over-year values. Last year, during week 12, spot cream multiples across All-Classes were 1.14 to 1.28, well above this week's range. Condensed skim demand was noted as strong this week, but planned downtime at some balancing plants next month is likely to keep condensed skim from moving into a tauter supply status. NOTE: Due to changes in the dairy industry, the Dairy Market News Fluid Milk and Cream - Central U.S. report will be realigning coverage of weekly cream price information. During the remainder of March 2025, Reports 11 - 14, Dairy Market News Central fluid report will include entries for Midwest cream prices. Beginning with Report 15, only Central cream prices will be included.

The spring flush season has begun in earnest in the Midwest and Northern Plains areas, while southern states in the Central region have been in flush conditions for over a month with generally mild temperatures before the summer heat begins. Fieldwork in the Upper Midwest is expected to ramp back up following last week's snow/freeze, but rain is expected in many areas of the Midwest this upcoming weekend. Milk component levels are steadily decreasing or holding steady week over week. Contacts suggest that there were years when 3.7 percent milkfat was a sought-after figure, when recent numbers are closer to and above 4.5 percent milkfat. Contacts say protein levels are closer to normal than those of milkfat. Fluid milk demand is mixed, as some Upper Midwest school districts are in the midst of their spring break, while schools in other parts of the region have returned. Cheesemakers are actively taking on spot milk, but there has been a recent growth in milk availability as plant downtime is keeping milk handlers busier week over week. Contacts say April will likely bring with it a number of notably sized plants being down for various updates, therefore milk and cream supplies are expected to remain in a surplus status through the spring holidays. Cream multiples ticked higher on the bottom of the range this week, as cream suppliers are saying they are finally able to breathe a sigh of relief ahead of further potential heaviness. Ice cream and cream cheese manufacturers are helping alleviate some of the excess cream loads on the market, but butter churning has been noticeably affected this year by the strong cream stores available since late 2024.NOTE: Due to changes in the dairy industry, the Dairy Market News Fluid Milk and Cream - Central U.S. report will be realigning coverage of weekly cream price information. During the remainder of March 2025, Reports 11 - 14, Dairy Market News Central fluid report will include entries for Midwest cream prices. Beginning with Report 15, only Central cream prices will be included.

Last year during week 14, early news of the Highly Pathogenic Avian Flu (HPAI) was being reported throughout the industry and throughout the country, but three Central region states were early on the list of infected dairy herds. HPAI in the Central region was and continues to be handily contained by farmers, veterinarians, and agricultural authorities. Milk output is on the rise, but not at a booming flush-like pace of yesteryear. Weather situations are typical to early spring and widely variant in the region. There was snow, rain, and tornadic activity in the north. Tornadic activity and strong windy/rainy days have been subjecting farmers in the south to distressing situations. That said, some Texas and Oklahoma farmers who provide feed to dairy farms say they are hopeful for more rainfall near term, as areas of western Oklahoma and the panhandle of Texas have been in drought-like conditions and under red flag warnings throughout the late winter and early spring. Class I pulls have been noticeably quieter in recent weeks. Since spring break, school district orders have been less consistent throughout the region. Cheesemakers are reporting stronger pulls into Class III production, though. With early flush levels of milk, and lighter Class I usage, cheesemakers are receiving more offers of spot milk and prices have dropped in the last week. Expectations are that milk markets will be similar next week, as both unexpected and scheduled plant downtime is commonplace in the Central region. Last year, during week 14, spot milk prices ranged from $5- to $1.50-under Class. Cream markets continued their recent trend this week. Cream volumes are far from tight, but cream suppliers' jobs have become more manageable over the past few weeks, as spot cream demand has gone from notably quiet to active due to bumps in needs from Class II and III manufacturers. All cream end users continue to say loads are widely available, but cream multiples continued to hold steady or edge slightly higher week over week. As recently as during week eight, cream multiples were reported at .5. April is expected to bring a lot of uncertainty to fluid milk and cream markets. Plant downtime is already creating some logistical headwinds for suppliers and haulers. With spring holidays approaching, more downtime will only add more hurdles as plants run lighter holiday schedules. Despite a very active spot milk trading week, cheesemakers say they are beginning to manage production schedules toward the lighter side, as customer demand has been on shaky ground in recent weeks and cheese inventories have grown.NOTE: Due to changes in the dairy industry, the Dairy Market News Fluid Milk and Cream - Central U.S. report will be realigning coverage of weekly cream price information. During the remainder of March 2025, Reports 11 - 14, Dairy Market News Central fluid report will include entries for Midwest cream prices. Beginning with Report 15, only Central cream prices will be included.

Some farmers have gotten ahead on tillage and small-grain planting in the Upper Midwest and North Plains. Still, though, some say soil is either not warm enough and/or fields are unworkably wet currently. Milk output continues to push higher week over week. The flush season has arrived and spot milk availability is evidence of the season. Bottlers are steadily taking in milk volumes as the schoolyear winds down, but not enough to keep spot milk prices at or above Class for processors. Cheesemakers reported more spot milk trading activity this week than in any other week so far this year. Spot milk prices ranged from $5-under to $1-under Class III. Some say expectations are that milk will remain available during and beyond lighter spring holiday work schedules. There is a new dynamic taking place in the south and southwest states within the Central region. Milk availability has tightened due to new cheese plants running more active schedules. In the Upper Midwest, though, milk volumes are becoming more available as plants continue to run on lighter schedules due primarily to updates and/or maintenance. Cream availability is in a somewhat steady trend with previous weeks, although loads may be slightly more available as spring holidays get closer. Butter makers continue to relay somewhat open availability, and cream handlers say they expect to be somewhat busy as they work ahead to find destinations for cream tanks throughout the rest of the month, but their particular focus will be on the latter half of next week. Ice cream and cream cheese manufacturers continue to help cream suppliers in their efforts, though, as Class II and Class III manufacturing needs continue to tick slightly higher week over week.The DMN monthly average of the range price series for Upper Midwest Class II cream during March was $2.4816, compared to $2.6892 in February.
